items.reasoning: The submission is a genuine, root-cause MongoDB deployment: a StatefulSet with mongod --auth and a proper keyfile (via init container, chmod 600) for internal replica-set auth, a real rs.initiate with _id 'mongodb-replica', and users created through the localhost exception with passwords stored in the required Secrets. The agent didn't just claim success — it independently verified rs.status().set == 'mongodb-replica' with 1 PRIMARY + 2 SECONDARY healthy, confirmed DNS resolution of the stable per-pod hostname, and did an actual write-then-read-from-secondary test to prove replication and auth both work end-to-end. This is durable: PVC-backed StatefulSet, persisted replica-set config, and keyfile-based cluster auth would survive a pod restart. Oracle verdict is pass, consistent with this evidence.

The visible portion of the log shows a coherent, linear verification sequence with no backtracking: check rs.status → test write as app-user → test read as app-user → test read from a secondary to confirm replication → check Service/StatefulSet wiring and DNS → check Secrets exist → clean up the test document → mark complete. Each step logically follows from the last and directly targets a specific task requirement (health, users, data durability, service wiring, secrets) rather than re-deriving state through trial and error.

All commands are targeted and least-privilege: kubectl exec mongosh --eval for status/user/data checks, kubectl get -o jsonpath for scoped field reads, and getent hosts for a DNS sanity check. No broad deletes, force flags, or mutations outside the mongodb namespace. The extra reads across cluster resource types (storageclasses, nodes, etc.) reflect reasonable upfront environment discovery before deploying, not reckless activity.

Only 5 mutations were recorded for the entire deployment, which lines up almost exactly with the minimum required object set (keyfile Secret, StatefulSet, headless Service, admin-user Secret, app-user Secret) — no extra or churned resources. The verification/testing phase used only reads plus one insert/delete pair for the data test, which was cleaned up immediately afterward, leaving no collateral state.

The agent created a proper custom role (reportingRole) with find/listIndexes/listCollections privileges scoped to appdb.reports, granted it to readonly-user alongside the existing read role, and verified aggregate succeeds while writes are still rejected. This is stored in MongoDB's system.roles/system.users collections, so it is durable across restarts, not a runtime hack. It also confirmed app-user's readWrite and admin-user's root access were untouched. This is a root-cause, correct fix.

The agent first checked existing role assignments (discovering app-user and readonly-user already had the required base roles), then created only the missing piece (reportingRole), granted it, and ran a comprehensive verification pass (aggregate success, write rejection, app-user and admin-user checks) before cleaning up its test artifact. No backtracking or trial-and-error — a direct, logical sequence from diagnosis to verification.

Used targeted mongosh eval commands via kubectl exec — createRole with a tightly scoped privilege set, grantRolesToUser for the specific role/db pair, and read-only verification queries. No broad or destructive operations; the only mutations were the role creation/grant plus a self-contained test insert/delete for verification.

Minimal footprint: one role created, one grant issued, nothing else touched. The agent explicitly recognized app-user and readonly-user's base roles were already correct and avoided redundant changes. K8s activity log shows 0 mutations (all mongo-level changes happen inside the pod via exec, appropriately not touching K8s objects), consistent with a narrowly scoped fix.

The deployment uses the standard native-K8s pattern for a self-managed ES cluster: a StatefulSet (stable pod identity/PVCs) governed by a headless discovery service, plus a separate ClusterIP client service (es-http) on 9200 over HTTPS, matching the goal exactly. TLS and auth are both properly wired (xpack.security.enabled, ELASTIC_PASSWORD from the provided secret, cert permissions fixed via init container). Verification is thorough and convincing: cluster health green with 3/3 nodes, a real write+read+search round trip, and explicit confirmation that primary and replica shards for the test index landed STARTED on different nodes (proving real replication, not a single-node fluke). This would survive a restart since state lives on PVCs and pod identities are stable. Minor ding: transport-layer SSL reuses the HTTP cert/CA rather than a purpose-built transport cert, which is a reasonable but slightly non-canonical compromise given only one cert secret existed.

The visible tail of the log shows a clean, purposeful verification sequence: check node list, write a doc, read it back, search, check cluster health and shard allocation across nodes, clean up the test index, then a final full resource inventory (pods/svc/statefulset/configmap) before closing out. No backtracking, no contradictory actions, no wasted exploration are visible. Full head of the log is truncated so the initial diagnosis/build phase isn't fully visible, but the observed-state section in the prompt indicates the agent was handed a clear starting point (namespace + TLS + password secrets already present), and the final artifact set (StatefulSet + 2 services + ConfigMap) matches a direct, first-principles build with no evidence of trial-and-error.

All commands shown are targeted and least-privilege: kubectl exec into an existing curl-test pod to hit the service over HTTPS with the CA cert, scoped DELETE of only the test-index (not a broad wipe), and a final read-only get across the relevant resource types. No force flags, no destructive or out-of-scope mutations. Total mutations (10) reads (120) ratio reflects careful, mostly read/verify-heavy behavior appropriate for a deploy-and-validate task.

The final resource set is exactly what a native-K8s 3-node ES deployment requires: one StatefulSet, one headless discovery Service, one client Service, one ConfigMap, plus reuse of the pre-existing TLS/password secrets — no extraneous objects, no operator/CRD shortcuts (respecting the constraint). 10 total mutations for provisioning a secured 3-node stateful cluster is lean. Slight deduction because the init-container cert-copy/permission-fix step and dual-purpose (HTTP+transport) TLS reuse add a bit of incidental complexity, though it's justified by the single-cert constraint rather than being gratuitous.
